Pandowdy Apple IIe Emulator
Table of Contents

Class VA2M

Namespace
Pandowdy.EmuCore
Assembly
Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll

Main Apple IIe emulator orchestrator coordinating CPU, bus, memory, and timing.

public class VA2M : IDisposable
Inheritance
object
VA2M
Implements
IDisposable
Inherited Members
object.Equals(object)
object.Equals(object, object)
object.GetHashCode()
object.GetType()
object.MemberwiseClone()
object.ReferenceEquals(object, object)
object.ToString()

Remarks

⚠️ PLANNED FOR REFACTORING: This class will be refactored to separate concerns (timing, input, state publishing) into dedicated services. See Pandowdy.EmuCore/_docs_/VA2MBus-Refactoring-Notes.md for planned architecture.

Name Origin: "VA2M" = "Virtual Apple II Machinator" - the machine orchestrator that brings together all emulator subsystems.

Threading Model:

  • Emulator Thread: Single-threaded CPU execution (Clock/RunAsync loop)
  • External Threads: UI/input threads enqueue commands via InjectKey(), etc.
  • Flash Timer: Separate timer thread toggles cursor at ~2.1 Hz
  • Synchronization: Commands processed at frame boundaries (thread-safe)

Execution Modes:

  • Clock(): Single-cycle execution, useful for debugging/stepping
  • RunAsync(): Continuous batched execution, normal operation mode

Constructors

VA2M(IEmulatorState, IFrameProvider, ISystemStatusProvider, IAppleIIBus, MemoryPool, IFrameGenerator)

Initializes a new instance of the VA2M emulator.

public VA2M(IEmulatorState stateSink, IFrameProvider frameSink, ISystemStatusProvider statusProvider, IAppleIIBus bus, MemoryPool memoryPool, IFrameGenerator frameGenerator)

Parameters

stateSink IEmulatorState

State provider for publishing emulator state snapshots.

frameSink IFrameProvider

Frame provider for publishing rendered video frames.

statusProvider ISystemStatusProvider

System status provider for soft switch states.

bus IAppleIIBus

System bus coordinating CPU, memory, and I/O.

memoryPool MemoryPool

Memory pool managing 128KB Apple IIe memory.

frameGenerator IFrameGenerator

Frame generator for rendering video output.

Remarks

Initialization Sequence:

  1. Store dependency references (all required)
  2. Load embedded Apple IIe ROM from resources
  3. Subscribe to VBlank event from bus (if VA2MBus)
  4. Start flash timer for cursor blinking

ROM Loading: The Apple IIe Enhanced ROM (16KB) is embedded as a resource and automatically loaded during construction. This ROM contains the Monitor, Applesoft BASIC, and peripheral firmware.

VBlank Event: If the bus is a VA2MBus, the OnVBlank handler is registered to trigger frame rendering and flash state updates at ~60 Hz.

Exceptions

ArgumentNullException

Thrown if any parameter is null.

Properties

Bus

Gets the system bus coordinating CPU, memory, and I/O access.

public IAppleIIBus Bus { get; }

Property Value

IAppleIIBus

MemoryPool

Gets the memory pool managing the 128KB Apple IIe memory space.

public MemoryPool MemoryPool { get; }

Property Value

MemoryPool

SystemClock

Gets the total number of CPU cycles executed since last reset.

public ulong SystemClock { get; }

Property Value

ulong

TargetHz

Gets or sets the target CPU frequency in Hz.

public double TargetHz { get; set; }

Property Value

double

Remarks

Default is 1,023,000 Hz (1.023 MHz), the Apple IIe's clock speed. Can be adjusted for testing or to match different Apple II models.

ThrottleEnabled

Gets or sets whether throttling is enabled to maintain Apple IIe speed.

public bool ThrottleEnabled { get; set; }

Property Value

bool

Remarks

When true, the emulator runs at ~1.023 MHz (Apple IIe speed). When false, runs as fast as possible (useful for loading programs, debugging).

Methods

Clock()

Advance one system clock (one CPU/bus cycle). If throttling is enabled, the call will delay to keep approx TargetHz. Suitable for simple loops.

public void Clock()

Remarks

Execution Sequence:

  1. Process pending cross-thread commands (ProcessPending)
  2. Execute one bus clock cycle (CPU + memory + I/O)
  3. Increment throttle cycle counter
  4. Apply throttling delay if enabled (ThrottleOneCycle)
  5. Publish emulator state snapshot

Use Cases:

  • Single-step debugging (execute one instruction at a time)
  • Simple synchronous execution loops
  • Testing and verification scenarios

Performance Note: For continuous operation, use RunAsync(CancellationToken, double) instead as it batches cycles to reduce per-cycle overhead.

Dispose()

Releases all resources used by the VA2M emulator instance.

public void Dispose()

Remarks

Cleanup Sequence:

  1. Stop and dispose flash timer (~2.1 Hz cursor blink timer)
  2. Clear pending command queue to release enqueued actions
  3. Dispose bus (includes VBlank event cleanup)
  4. Suppress finalization (no unmanaged resources)

Thread Safety: Should be called from the thread that owns the emulator instance (typically the UI thread after stopping emulation). Do not call while RunAsync() is executing.

MemoryPool Note: MemoryPool disposal is currently commented out as it may be shared across multiple emulator instances or have external ownership. This should be revisited if MemoryPool lifetime is clarified.

CPU Note: The legacy 6502.NET CPU library does not implement IDisposable, so no explicit CPU cleanup is needed. The CPU instance is managed by the Bus.

InjectKey(byte)

Inject a keyboard value into the machine as if a key was latched at $C000. High bit must be set. Cleared by access of $C010.

public void InjectKey(byte ascii)

Parameters

ascii byte

ASCII character code (0-127). High bit will be set automatically.

Remarks

Apple IIe Keyboard Protocol: The Apple IIe keyboard latch is a memory-mapped register at $C000. When a key is pressed, the ASCII value with bit 7 set appears at this address. Reading $C010 (KBDSTRB) clears bit 7, indicating the key has been read.

Thread Safety: This method is thread-safe. It enqueues the key injection command which will be executed on the emulator thread at the next ProcessPending() call. This allows UI threads to inject keyboard input without race conditions.

ASCII Codes:

  • 0x20-0x7E: Standard printable ASCII characters
  • 0x0D (13): Return/Enter key
  • 0x08 (8): Backspace (sometimes)
  • 0x15 (21): Right arrow (Apple IIe convention)
  • 0x1B (27): Escape

Implementation: The method automatically sets bit 7 (ORs with 0x80) to match Apple IIe hardware behavior. The command is enqueued and executed via Bus.SetKeyValue() on the emulator thread.

Reset()

Reset machine and system clock.

public void Reset()

Remarks

Full System Reset (Power Cycle): This method performs a complete hardware reset equivalent to powering off and on the Apple IIe.

Operations Performed:

  • Reset CPU registers (PC loaded from $FFFC/$FFFD, SP = $FF)
  • Reset all soft switches to power-on state
  • Reset memory bank mappings
  • Clear keyboard latch
  • Reset cycle counter to zero
  • Restart throttle stopwatch
  • Reset performance measurement counters

Difference from UserReset: Reset() is a cold boot that initializes everything. UserReset() is a warm reset that preserves memory contents and only resets the CPU (Ctrl+Reset behavior).

Thread Safety: Should be called from the emulator thread. For cross-thread reset, enqueue via command queue.

RunAsync(CancellationToken, double)

Run the emulator asynchronously with batched cycles and time slices. Batches cycles per tick (e.g.,1 ms or 60 Hz) to reduce overhead of per-cycle waits. When ThrottleEnabled is true, pacing uses the periodic timer to approximate TargetHz. When false, runs fast batches without waiting.

public Task RunAsync(CancellationToken ct, double ticksPerSecond = 1000)

Parameters

ct CancellationToken

Cancellation token to stop the runner.

ticksPerSecond double

Time slice frequency. Use 1000 for 1ms slices or 60 for video-frame pacing.

Returns

Task

A task that completes when the cancellation token is triggered or the emulator stops.

Remarks

Throttled Mode (ThrottleEnabled = true):

  • Uses PeriodicTimer to wait for each tick (e.g., 1ms or ~16.67ms for 60 Hz)
  • Executes calculated number of cycles per tick (TargetHz / ticksPerSecond)
  • Accumulates fractional cycles to prevent drift over time
  • Processes pending commands before each batch
  • Publishes state after each batch

Fast Mode (ThrottleEnabled = false):

  • Executes 10,000 cycle batches as fast as possible
  • No timer delays (runs full CPU speed)
  • Yields with Task.Delay(0) to remain responsive
  • Useful for loading programs quickly or testing

Recommended ticksPerSecond Values:

  • 1000: 1ms time slices, ~1,023 cycles/tick (balanced)
  • 60: ~16.67ms slices, ~17,050 cycles/tick (matches VBlank)
  • 100: 10ms slices, ~10,230 cycles/tick (less frequent)

Fractional Cycle Accumulation: The algorithm tracks fractional cycles with a carry variable to prevent cumulative drift. For example, with ticksPerSecond=1000, each tick should execute 1023 cycles, but the fractional 0.0 carry is accumulated so over time the average is exactly 1,023,000 Hz.

Cancellation: The loop checks the cancellation token after each batch and breaks cleanly. OperationCanceledException is caught and handled gracefully.

ConfigureAwait(false): Used to avoid capturing synchronization context, improving performance and preventing potential deadlocks in UI scenarios.

Exceptions

ArgumentOutOfRangeException

Thrown if ticksPerSecond is zero or negative.

SetPushButton(byte, bool)

Sets the state of an Apple IIe pushbutton (game controller button).

public void SetPushButton(byte num, bool pressed)

Parameters

num byte

Button number (0-2). Button 0 is typically button 0, buttons 1-2 are paddle buttons.

pressed bool

True if button is pressed, false if released.

Remarks

Apple IIe Pushbutton Hardware: The Apple IIe has three pushbutton inputs typically used for game controllers (joysticks/paddles). These are read from I/O addresses $C061-$C063.

Button Mapping:

  • Button 0 ($C061): Typically joystick/paddle button 0
  • Button 1 ($C062): Typically joystick/paddle button 1
  • Button 2 ($C063): Typically joystick/paddle button 2

Reading Buttons: Software reads the button state by checking bit 7 of the corresponding I/O address. If bit 7 is set, the button is pressed.

Thread Safety: This method is thread-safe. It enqueues the button state change which will be executed on the emulator thread at the next ProcessPending() call.

UserReset()

Performs a warm reset (Ctrl+Reset) without clearing memory or resetting cycle counters.

public void UserReset()

Remarks

Warm Reset Behavior: Simulates pressing Ctrl+Reset on the Apple IIe, which resets the CPU but preserves RAM contents and continues timing.

Operations Performed:

  • Reset CPU registers (PC loaded from $FFFC/$FFFD, SP reset)
  • Memory contents preserved (RAM, soft switches mostly unchanged)
  • Cycle counter continues (not reset)
  • Throttle timing continues (not restarted)

Use Cases:

  • Break out of infinite loop without losing program in memory
  • Return to Monitor/BASIC prompt from running program
  • Recover from program hang while preserving state

Difference from Reset: Reset() is a cold boot (power cycle). UserReset() is a warm reset that preserves memory and timing state.

Thread Safety: This method is thread-safe. It enqueues the reset operation which will be executed on the emulator thread at the next instruction boundary (via ProcessPending). This ensures the reset doesn't interrupt a CPU instruction in progress, maintaining 6502 atomic instruction guarantees.

Implementation Note: The reset command is enqueued and will be processed at the next safe point (instruction boundary) on the emulator thread. This prevents race conditions and undefined behavior from resetting the CPU mid-instruction.

Edit this page
